danger_signals.visual_form: Animated enemy sprites moving through maze corridors
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat to player; collision causes damage/death

danger_signals.visual_form: Explosion sprite with expanding blast radius animation
danger_signals.meaning: Area-of-effect danger; destroys walls, kills enemies, harms player if caught in radius

danger_signals.visual_form: Soft blocks (visually distinct from hard perimeter walls) scattered throughout maze
danger_signals.meaning: Can be destroyed by bombs to reveal items or create pathways; hiding spots for enemies

danger_signals.visual_form: Sequential explosions propagating across multiple bombs
danger_signals.meaning: Uncontrolled escalation of danger; loss of tactical control

safety_signals.visual_form: Solid outer border blocks and fixed internal structures
safety_signals.meaning: Impassable barriers that cannot be destroyed; provide safe cover from explosions

safety_signals.visual_form: Clear tile separation between player and enemies/explosions
safety_signals.meaning: Spatial buffer reducing immediate threat; allows escape planning

safety_signals.visual_form: Designated portal tile (revealed after all enemies defeated)
safety_signals.meaning: Safe progression point; signals completion of stage

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Operates within maze corridors; controls bomb placement

faction_indicators.color_identity: Varied enemy sprite colors (distinct from player)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Different enemy types have unique appearance and movement patterns
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Patrol maze corridors; emerge from destructible wall tiles; constrained by hard walls

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Multiple enemy sprites visible in maze; exit door hidden/not displayed; soft blocks intact
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies patrol randomly through corridors using complex AI; player must avoid and eliminate

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Exit door becomes visible/revealed on screen; remaining soft blocks show as ordinary tiles
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can safely walk to door to progress; no active threats in maze

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Bomb sprite on tile with visual/audio countdown indicator
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Explosion occurs after set frames; blast propagates orthogonally until blocked by hard walls

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Hidden bonus item tile (Goddess Mask, Cola Bottle, Famicom, etc.) briefly revealed in debug mode as red half-destroyed wall
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player has ~8.5 seconds to locate and collect before disappearance; grants massive point bonus

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Player sprite death animation; removal from screen
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Loss of life/stage restart; visual feedback confirms damage resolution

environmental_storytelling.example: Each of 50 stages presents differently-arranged hard walls and soft block placement, creating unique tactical puzzles that suggest progression in difficulty and complexity

Bonus items referenced to Hudson Soft games (Star Force sprite, Dezeni World character portrait, programmer Nakamoto-san) embedded as ultra-rare unlocks, creating metagame narrative of developer self-reference
Random AI movement through procedurally-generated level layout creates perception of intelligent threat; complex random function prevents pattern recognition, maintaining tension
Exit remains invisible until all enemies cleared, visually encoding the narrative: 'escape is only possible after threat elimination'

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Move through corridors using pseudo-random pathing; reverse direction when hitting walls; spawn from destroyed soft blocks
Sprite disappears; removed from threat pool; signals progress toward stage completion
Remains stationary; counts down visually/aurally; cannot be moved by player after placement
Explosion animates orthogonally (up/down/left/right) until blocked by hard wall; soft blocks destroyed; enemies killed; player damaged if in blast
Occupies tile; blocks movement; can hide items or enemies; destroyable by bombs
Sprite removed; tile becomes passable; potential item revealed; potential enemy spawn point
Navigates corridors via D-Pad input; constrained to grid movement; cannot occupy soft block or hard wall tiles
Places bomb on current tile; can only hold one active bomb at a time (unless bonus items modify); creates tactical pressure

notes: Bomberman NES (1985/1987) establishes the core franchise visual language through minimal sprite-based communication. The game relies on clear tile-state signaling: walls are barriers, soft blocks are destroyables, enemies are moving threats, bombs are time-delayed hazards, and the hidden exit encodes narrative closure. No faction politics or political situational complexity present; this is pure abstract puzzle-action, not a world-state narrative game. Environmental storytelling emerges from meta-references (developer credits as bonus items) rather than world-narrative. The manifestation layer communicates purely through mechanical state: what you see is what mechanical rules allow. Danger signals are isomorphic with game hazards; safety signals are simply absence of hazard. Behavioral tells directly map to game state—enemies move because they're alive, sprites vanish because state changes. The 50-stage progression itself is the only long-form world-state arc.
